stories.title: The Recycling Project
stories.story:
When our school announced a new recycling competition between classes, most students groaned — it sounded boring compared to sports day. But our teacher, Ms. Farida, challenged us to think differently. "Whoever collects the most recyclable materials in a month wins a class trip," she announced. Suddenly, everyone was interested.

Our class decided to split into teams, each responsible for a different material: paper, plastic bottles, and aluminum cans. I was assigned to the plastics team with my friend Sami. At first, we struggled to collect much — most families already used most of their plastic before throwing it away.

Sami had an idea. "What if we ask local shops to save their plastic packaging for us instead of throwing it away?" We spent our weekend visiting nearby shops, explaining our project, and most shop owners were happy to help once they understood it was for a good cause.

By the end of the month, our class had collected an enormous amount of recyclable material — so much that the recycling center sent a truck specifically for our school. We didn't just win the trip; we learned that small individual choices, multiplied across a whole community, could create a real difference for the environment. Ms. Farida told us the recycling center said our materials would be reused instead of sitting in a landfill for hundreds of years.

stories.title: The Debate Club
stories.story:
Adam had always been the quiet kid in class, the one who never raised his hand even when he knew the answer. So when his teacher suggested he join the school debate club, Adam laughed nervously. "I could never speak in front of people," he said.

His teacher didn't push him, but she mentioned that the debate club was looking for someone who genuinely cared about environmental issues, since Adam had written the best essay in class about ocean pollution. Something about that topic made Adam consider it — he really did care about the ocean, more than he cared about being embarrassed.

At his first debate club meeting, Adam's hands shook as he practiced arguing his position with the other members. It felt terrifying at first, but the club leader reminded everyone that debate wasn't about being the loudest — it was about having well-researched, thoughtful arguments. Adam realized his careful, quiet way of thinking was actually a strength in debates, not a weakness.

Three months later, Adam stood on stage at the regional debate competition, arguing passionately about protecting marine life. His voice was steady, his facts were solid, and for the first time, being quiet and thoughtful felt like exactly the right way to be. Adam's team didn't win the whole competition, but he had discovered a version of himself he never knew existed.
